original_abstract: This article investigates the historical origins and conceptual foundations of the orthodox Trinitarian formula, primarily seeking to determine the extent to which the Nicene doctrine was shaped by Greek philosophical thought. By engaging with contemporary theological discourse, the study compares the traditional Hellenistic formulations of the Trinity against the biblical revelation of the Godhead. The research concludes that the Trinitarian formula may be better understood not as a rigid dogmatic construct, but as a profound mystery—a perspective that invites a "de-hellenization" of the concept. Ultimately, the paper argues that the Church Fathers and the post-Nicene Church adopted Greek philosophical and theological terminology largely for the purpose of contextualization, drawing upon doxological and liturgical themes already nascent within the Scriptures.
humanized_abstract: Can a biblical doctrine of God have a Greek inheritance? The article explores the origins and components of the Orthodox formula of the Trinity in an attempt to decide the extent to which the Nicene understanding of God is coloured by Greek philosophy and so contrasts traditional Hellenistic Trinitarian formulations with the biblical revelation of God as Trinity. With close engagement of the current discussion in dogmatics, the article assesses whether the Trinitarian formula should be regarded as a mystery rather than a dogmatic formula and hence needs “dehellenization.” This means that the terms and concepts used by the Church Fathers and after Nicaea were employed not for explanatory reasons but merely as a contextualization tool using doxological and liturgical terms, some of which can be traced back to Scripture.
